nodes.intent: A deployment that cannot be traced, reproduced, or validated from zero is not a deployment — every change travels through scaffolded workflows with config committed to the repo, validates through a prod-identical test hierarchy with branch-gated promotion, and the full platform provisions from zero and returns to zero without human access, verified weekly.

Any platform resource with a fixed cost floor bills whether work happens or not — platform resources must use free-tier SKUs only, and each application's spend flows to a dedicated invoice section, never pooled.

A security requirement without a hard control is a convention — every boundary is enforced by Azure Policy Deny at management group scope regardless of the resource's default posture, with self-service exemptions as the only escape path.

Every principal that touches the platform is an Entra ID identity — no local accounts, no static secrets, no individual role bindings. Authentication is federated, access is group-scoped, and each landing zone owns its identity boundary.

Sharing infrastructure between landing zones couples their lifecycles — each zone gets its own subscription, identity, VNet, monitoring, and automation runtime so teams have full operational control inside a boundary the platform creates.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy denies resource types not on the platform-approved list.
nodes.why: An Azure resource absent from the list has no security baseline or diagnostic coverage.

nodes.mechanism: An application must be registered before landing zones are provisioned — establishes billing scope, access groups, and repository.
nodes.why: without this, billing per application, and landing zone self-service and autonomy wouldn't exist.

nodes.mechanism: Budget alerts use forecast thresholds — single alert detects spend trajectory regardless of consumption level.
nodes.why: Actual-spend thresholds (50%, 80%) fire on normal consumption variability — noise without decision value. A single forecast threshold detects trend deviations regardless of spend pattern.

nodes.mechanism: Each application has a dedicated billing scope. All its subscriptions bill to it — cost attribution is structural, not inferred from tags.
nodes.why: Shared invoice sections make it impossible to attribute a cost anomaly to a single team without manual analysis.

nodes.mechanism: Only ARM-reachable services are eligible — Policy, RBAC, Deployment Stacks, and diagnostic settings are all ARM-native controls.
nodes.why: Non Azure Resource Manager resources have no policy, RBAC, or diagnostic coverage, leaving control plane blind for platform

nodes.mechanism: Every production deployment requires prior validation in a test environment — enforced, not conventional.
nodes.why: Without an enforced gate, a single push can change production infrastructure with no prior validation.

nodes.mechanism: Configuration has one committed source of truth — propagated to runtime at deployment, never set manually.
nodes.why: Without one committed source, environments drift and onboarding means tracing values through the GitHub UI.

nodes.mechanism: When no built-in policy fulfills the required effect, a custom definition is authored to close the gap.
nodes.why: Without a custom definition, a resource type missing built-in Deny or diagnostic coverage cannot be whitelisted.

nodes.mechanism: All Deployment Stacks use deleteAll lifecycle. Any Azure resource not declared in the Bicep template is deleted on the next deployment — the template is the source of truth.
nodes.why: Without deleteAll, orphaned resources persist with no code representation and decommissioning means manual auditing.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy denies cross-tenant object replication.
nodes.why: Replication is a data-plane operation network policies don't see, so it needs a separate deny.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy denies local authentication. Exceptions require a policy exemption.
nodes.why: A key or connection string is an identity outside Entra — unseen by RBAC, audit, and Conditional Access.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy denies public network access. Exceptions require a policy exemption.
nodes.why: Without a deny, accidental public exposure looks identical to intentional and acquires attack surface silently.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy denies TLS versions below 1.2. Exceptions require a policy exemption.
nodes.why: Without an enfordment standards, traffic can be silently downgraded to a cryptographically weak TLS version.

nodes.mechanism: Gating and execution are separate layers — triggers own conditions and gates, execution layers own logic. Never merged.
nodes.why: Mixing gating with execution forces the same deploy logic to be duplicated per environment.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone owns its generated parameter file and workflow — no artifacts are shared between zones.
nodes.why: Without a dedicated workflow per landing zone, deployment lifecycles are coupled — one zone's change affects another's

nodes.mechanism: Landing zones are discoverable by naming convention alone. No central list is maintained.
nodes.why: Without the prefix contract, a hardcoded list needs an entry per zone and fails silently when one is missing.

nodes.mechanism: All infrastructure changes travel through PR → review → GitHub Actions → Bicep pipeline — no change reaches Azure outside it.
nodes.why: A change made outside the pipeline has no code representation — invisible to review and deleted on the next deploy.

nodes.mechanism: Pipelines authenticate via short-lived federated tokens — no static secrets stored, each token scoped to repo and environment.
nodes.why: Static secrets outlive the job and can be extracted; OIDC tokens expire with the run.

nodes.mechanism: Azure roles assigned to Entra ID groups, not individuals. Platform owns groups and role bindings; membership managed in Entra ID.
nodes.why: Without groups, access management escapes Entra ID into infrastructure code — access reviews, and audit trails no longer apply to platform access.

nodes.mechanism: A single Entra ID app registration serves as the platform identity — manages platform resources, creates landing zones, and assigns Directory.Read.All to each LZ identity.
nodes.why: Managed identities need an Azure resource to exist — app registrations are pure Entra ID objects that can exist before any subscription.

nodes.mechanism: A single GitHub App serves as the platform's GitHub-side identity — used by all platform workflows and landing zone deployments to authenticate to GitHub.
nodes.why: Without a dedicated GitHub identity, cross-repo and org-level operations have no viable auth path.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone identity gets Directory.Read.All to query users, groups, and principals in Entra ID.
nodes.why: Without Directory.Read.All, LZ identities cannot query Entra ID — group membership, user lookups, and principal resolution all fail.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zone creation automatically allocates each VNet a unique CIDR from the platform managed address space. No CIDR is assigned manually
nodes.why: Fixing an address overlap means re-addressing — destroying and recreating every resource holding an IP.

nodes.mechanism: Custom roles are scoped to job function, not resource type. When new capabilities require new actions, existing roles are extended — not new ones created
nodes.why: Resource-specific roles multiply with every new type until auditing them becomes impractical.

nodes.mechanism: A dedicated Action Group per landing zone wires alerts to contact details sourced from the application profile.
nodes.why: Hardcoded contacts go stale as teams change — alerts fire to former members, incidents go unanswered

nodes.mechanism: Automation jobs are control-plane only — data-plane access and fixed-cost image hosting are prohibited.
nodes.why: VNet integration carries a fixed monthly cost — without it, data-plane access is unavailable and a private registry is unreachable.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has a dedicated automation runtime — centrally managed, running inside the landing zone's subscription.
nodes.why: Centralized automation requires a shared management subscription with baseline cost and team dependency — the platform has none.

nodes.mechanism: Pipelines must execute inside the landing zone network.
nodes.why: Public runners require public data-plane exposure — Azure Policy denies that, blocking all data-plane pipeline jobs.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone has a single managed identity that serves all its capabilities — workflows, automation jobs, and Entra ID queries.
nodes.why: Without a single identity, each capability needs its own RBAC and OIDC config — permission surface multiplies with every new capability.

nodes.mechanism: A dedicated Log Analytics workspace per landing zone with 30-day retention.
nodes.why: A shared workspace blends ingestion cost across zones — no single team owns a line on the bill.

nodes.mechanism: All platform-managed landing zone resources live in a single resource group, and protected by Azure Deployment Stack from unwanted actions.
nodes.why: A shared resource group blends platform and application resources — no clean ownership boundary exists for teardown, RBAC, or incident management.

nodes.mechanism: Each landing zone gets a dedicated VNet with a CIDR from IPAM. PaaS services connect via service endpoints — private endpoints are not used because they add fixed hourly cost.
nodes.why: Without a dedicated VNet, network routing and configuration become shared decisions requiring cross-team coordination.

nodes.mechanism: Each platform capability has a dedicated workflow that owns its complete deployment. No capability's resources are provisioned outside its own workflow.
nodes.why: Without per-capability deployment, changes accumulate circular dependencies that force coordinated redeploys.

nodes.mechanism: Azure Policy enforces required tags on every resource. Values come from the application profile — not from whoever creates the resource.
nodes.why: Tags left to whoever creates a resource drift and go missing, so incident routing fails.

nodes.mechanism: Environment tier is a lifecycle label, not a placement control — all landing zones share one hierarchy.
nodes.why: Without uniform placement, dev and staging landing zones fall under different policy and RBAC — platform rules become tier-dependent.

nodes.mechanism: Platform changes deploy to a prod-identical test hierarchy before reaching production — environment where failures are safe to land.
nodes.why: Without a parallel hierarchy, platform changes hit production directly — failures land on live landing zones.

nodes.mechanism: Policy assignment ARM IDs are never hardcoded — a generated reference is the single resolution path.
nodes.why: Without the generated file, assignment IDs are hardcoded from a portal lookup — they drift silently when an assignment is redeployed and its ARM ID changes.

nodes.mechanism: Assignment names must encode enforcement effect and compliance domain — posture legible from the name alone.
nodes.why: Without the prefix, a reviewer can't tell from the name whether an assignment denies, audits, or modifies resources.

nodes.mechanism: The platform applies a fixed set of Defender for Cloud exemptions to every landing zone — recommendations with no free remediation path.
nodes.why: Excluded recommendations have no free remediation path — exempting them removes noise from the compliance score.

nodes.mechanism: Policy exemptions are declared in code by application teams — no operator approval, no portal creation.
nodes.why: Without self-service, exemption decisions shift to operators who lack the application-specific context to evaluate them.

nodes.mechanism: Control plane policy enforces region boundaries independently of pipeline configuration.
nodes.why: The pipeline's region setting is bypassable by portal or script; Azure policy enforces it at the control plane.

nodes.mechanism: Region is defined once and injected everywhere — engineers never specify it. Pipeline, Policy, and VNet stay in sync automatically.
nodes.why: Without a platform-managed region, engineers make geographic decisions manually — every deployment, every service, every landing zone.

nodes.mechanism: Landing zone provisioning draws from a 'subscription bank'. Decommissioned subscriptions go into a bank — new ones are created only when the bank is empty.
nodes.why: MCA quota counts active and cancelled subscriptions, so always creating new subscription exhausts quota ceiling.

nodes.mechanism: Every approved resource type has diagnostic settings deployed automatically by Azure Policy. Adding a type to the approved list requires adding its diagnostic coverage.
nodes.why: Without diagnostic settings defined per approved type, telemetry depends on each team remembering to enable it.
